arguments.task: Open official Salesforce first-party pages needed to verify API toolkit buildability. Read and extract evidence from canonical pages for: product/API description, Salesforce API types (REST/GraphQL/Both), authentication methods documented for API access, pricing or access requirements for developer/API use, rate limit notes, and whether Salesforce offers an official MCP server. Prefer developer.salesforce.com, help.salesforce.com, and salesforce.com first-party pages. Return the exact URLs visited and concise supporting quotes or facts for each field. If a page is JS-rendered, wait for content to load. Do not use third-party sources or search-result snippets.

input.thought: Start a browser research run on official Salesforce sources.

arguments.task: Open and read this official Salesforce authentication documentation page and any directly linked first-party pages needed to verify all documented auth methods for Salesforce API access. Determine whether the docs support only OAuth 2.0 or also another first-party documented method such as username-password flow, JWT bearer flow, client credentials, SOAP login, session ID, or personal/API token. Only count methods that are explicitly documented on first-party developer/API pages for API authentication. Also verify a first-party rate limit page and an official Salesforce MCP server page if available. Return exact canonical URLs visited and concise supporting quotes/facts for auth methods, rate limits, and MCP existence.

input.thought: Run a focused second browser pass for auth, rate limits, and MCP evidence on official Salesforce pages.

raw_result.description: CRM platform with APIs for accessing and managing Salesforce data, metadata, and connected workflows.
raw_result.auth_notes: Official Salesforce REST API auth docs state API access uses OAuth 2.0 via connected apps/external client apps. Salesforce also documents a SOAP API login call that returns a session ID used to authenticate subsequent API requests, which maps here to a non-standard scheme ('other'). I did not find first-party evidence for API keys, Basic auth, or personal access tokens in the gathered pages.
raw_result.gating_reason: Official REST API edition docs say API access is enabled by default only for Enterprise, Unlimited, Performance, and Developer Editions, while Professional Edition requires purchasing API access separately. This means production API access generally requires a paid Salesforce plan, even though Developer Edition exists for development/testing.

api_surface.breadth: Broad official API surface with core REST API plus GraphQL API, alongside multiple platform APIs surfaced from the Salesforce developer API catalog.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Salesforce’s official developer navigation includes a Model Context Protocol (MCP) section, indicating an official Salesforce MCP server/resource exists.

raw_result.blocker: Developer Edition supports building and testing, but real customer deployments typically require a paid Salesforce edition with API access enabled; access can also vary by edition/add-on.
raw_result.rate_limit_notes: Salesforce documents API limits, but the specific numerical limits were not captured in the gathered evidence. Limits are edition/org dependent and should be confirmed from the official API limits documentation before implementation.
raw_result.notable_signals: Strong first-party developer documentation, dedicated REST and GraphQL docs, and edition-specific API access guidance indicate a mature platform for agent/toolkit development.
raw_result.human_review_reason: The structured field mcp_exists is not fully supported by a directly read canonical MCP page in the gathered evidence; the conclusion is inferred from developer-site navigation mentions rather than a verified standalone official MCP document. Rate-limit specifics were also not captured, though that does not itself drive the structured review flag.
